title: ROMINA PROPERTIES LIMITED and WINKFORD FARM LIMITED and PARSONAGE FARM LIMITED
text:
IN THE MATTER of the Companies Acts 1931 芒聙聯 1993
and
IN THE MATTER of ROMINA PROPERTIES LIMITED (in voluntary liquidation)
and
IN THE MATTER of Winkford Farm Limited (in voluntary liquidation)
and
IN THE MATTER of Parsonage Farm Limited (in voluntary liquidation)
and
IN THE MATTER of the Intended Action in the High Court of Justice of England and Wales

Petition




[1] By Petition dated 1st June 2004, Steven George Taylor (the Liquidator), in his capacity as liquidator of Romina Properties Limited (Romina), Winkford Farms Limited (Winkford) and Parsonage Farms Limited (Parsonage) (together referred to as the Companies), seeks that this Court sanction the terms of an Intended Compromise Agreement (Compromise Agreement) and/or authorise the Liquidator to enter into and implement the terms of the Compromise Agreement.




Representation




[2] At the hearing, Mr. P.B. Clucas appeared for the Liquidator, Mr. P.D. Morris for the Islamic Republic of Pakistan (Government of Pakistan), and Mr. A. White QC with Mr. J.T. Aycock for Mr. Asif Ali Zardari.




General Background




[3] On 2nd October 1995, Romina purchased Rockwood House, Witley, Surrey, England, with 355 acres or thereabouts for 拢2,225,000. I shall refer to the whole of the hereditaments, and lands so purchased as the 芒聙聹Rockwood Estate芒聙聺.




[4] Following such purchase, Rockwood Estate was divided into three parcels with Rockwood House and land associated therewith being owned by Romina, Winkford Farm, and lands by Winkford, and Parsonage Farm, and lands by Parsonage.




[5] On 8th February 2002, Romina, Winkford, and Parsonage were each placed into members芒聙聶 voluntary liquidation with the Liquidator being appointed liquidator of each such company. In the course of the liquidations the Liquidator has received claims from various creditors, some of which have been paid in full or part, or accepted in full or part.




[6] By the Petition, the Liquidator pleads that the liquidations of Romina, Winkford, and Parsonage arose following assertions by the Government of Pakistan that Rockwood Estate was beneficially owned by Mrs. Benazir Bhutto and/or her husband, Mr. Zardari, and that the monies used to finance such purchase were provided through Bomer Finance Inc. by Mrs. Bhutto and/or Mr. Zardari. The Petition discloses the Liquidator芒聙聶s belief that the monies used to effect such purchase were provided from a bank account or accounts numbered 0/342035/019 and 0/342035/027 in the name of Bomer Finance Inc., a company incorporated on 25th April 1991 in the British Virgin Islands. The Liquidator also pleads that consequent upon assertions that such financing emanated from alleged illegal acts of corruption, the Government of Pakistan made various claims against the Rockwood Estate and/or the Companies, either as a creditor or by way of proprietary claim. The Liquidator further pleads that the Government of Pakistan has indicated that it will take action in the English courts, by which it will seek declarations that it is beneficially entitled to the Rockwood Estate, and all other assets of the Companies as are found to be derived directly, or indirectly from monies received by, or for the benefit of Mrs. Bhutto, and/or Mr. Zardari as secret commissions, and/or bribes; that Rockwood Estate, and such other assets are held upon constructive trusts for the Government of Pakistan; that the net proceeds of sale of the Rockwood Estate be given to the Government of Pakistan, and other appropriate relief. The Liquidator pleads that he believes that, if the Government of Pakistan succeeds in such intended action, there will be no assets to meet creditors芒聙聶 claims, and the Companies would be insolvent. Thus, he further pleads that on 4th November 2003, he entered into the Compromise Agreement with the Government of Pakistan, which agreement is expressed to be conditional upon approval by this Court. The Liquidator sets out his reasons for seeking the Court芒聙聶s sanction at paragraph 22 of the Petition.




[7] The Liquidator pleads that as well as its claim against Rockwood Estate, and the assets of the Companies, the Government of Pakistan has a claim for funding it has provided to the Liquidator in course of the liquidations under clause 5 of the Compromise Agreement.




[8] The Petition is supported by the Liquidator芒聙聶s affirmation (the Liquidator芒聙聶s first affirmation) sworn on 2nd June 2004 with exhibits thereto, in which affirmation he refers in some detail to the history of the matter, including certain proceedings in this, and other jurisdictions.




[9] In general terms, by his Answer, Mr. Zardari denies that the Rockwood Estate is beneficially owned by Mrs. Bhutto; that the Government of Pakistan would be successful in any action to obtain its desired declarations and relief; and any allegation of corruption. He maintains that each such allegation cannot be properly adjudicated upon in this jurisdiction, and pleads that such allegations are the subject of proceedings in Pakistan, and Switzerland. He pleads that the Liquidator has not seen any, or a substantial part of the material in his defence, and that the Liquidator cannot reach conclusions on matters of which he, or his advisers have no expertise. Mr. Zardari asserts that the Compromise Agreement is invalid under the laws of Pakistan; and denies that the Liquidator is entitled to the relief sought. Mr. Zardari seeks that the Petition is dismissed or stayed; that this Court orders that legitimate creditors be paid out of the proceeds of sale of the Rockwood Estate; that the Liquidator provides an account of his fees, and expenses; and that the net proceeds of the liquidations be paid into court pending further order.




[10] In his Answer, Mr. Zardari, being at the material time incarcerated in Pakistan, refers to the first affidavit (Mr. Bakar芒聙聶s first affidavit) sworn on 26th October 2004 of Abu Bakar, an advocate retained by Mr. Zardari, in which Mr. Bakar refers to various proceedings, challenges the basis upon which the liquidations were commenced, and challenges the Compromise Agreement. Mr. Bakar further challenges the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s allegations of corruption, the conduct of the Liquidator, and the funding of the liquidations. 




[11] By its Answer, the Government of Pakistan supports the Petition, and the relief sought.




[12] By his Reply, the Liquidator pleads that Mr. Zardari, having failed to identify any personal interest in the Companies, and/or the Rockwood Estate, and/or the proceeds of sale thereof, is not a person capable of being affected by this Court芒聙聶s decisions; that no creditor, or contributory opposes the Petition; and takes issue with matters pleaded in Mr. Zardari芒聙聶s Answer.




[13] By its Answer, and Cross Petition, Tower Trustees Limited pleads that it is the trustee of the Winkford and Parsonage Farm Trust, which was created by deed on 6th September 1995; that the sole named beneficiary is the International Red Cross of Geneva; and that the trustee holds, through Bernard Michael Shimmin, the whole of the issued share capitals in Winkford, and Parsonage, which shares are the sole assets of the trust. The Answer records that, following execution of the Compromise Agreement, Tower Trustees Limited, and /or Mr Shimmin as shareholder in Winkford, and Parsonage were approached to consider sanctioning such agreement; that Tower Trustees Limited indicated that it would consider such request , but before doing so intended to give notice to certain interested, or potentially interested parties, including Mr. Zardari. The Answer pleads that, having had benefit of the pleadings with affidavits, and affirmations in support, Tower Trustees Limited proposes to adopt a neutral stance in these proceedings, to comply with any orders for discovery, to rely on this Court芒聙聶s decision; and to seek that its costs be borne as part of the payments under the Compromise Agreement, or by another party to these proceedings.




[14] By their respective Answers to the Tower Trustees Limited芒聙聶s Cross Petition, the Liquidator raises no objection to the payment of the requested costs, but the Government of Pakistan, and Mr. Zardari opposes the same




[15] On 31st May 2005, this Court ordered that Tower Trustees Limited is at liberty to adopt a neutral stance, and to take no active part in these proceedings. I record that Mr Shimmin, and the directors of Winkford, and Parsonage have also adopted a neutral stance. I further record that Legibus Nominees Limited, and Legibus Trust Limited, the shareholders in Romina, have been given notice of these proceedings, but have taken no part therein.




[16] On 16th July 2004, the Liquidator sold the Rockwood Estate, and received net proceeds in the sum of 拢4,206,781.85 after payment of legal, and estate agents fees.






Compromise Agreement and Side Letter




[17] I pause briefly to refer to the provisions of the Compromise Agreement, which recites that the Liquidator has received formal detailed notice of the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s claim to the Rockwood Estate by means of draft Particulars of Claim; and that from the Companies芒聙聶 perspectives the Liquidator is satisfied that the agreement represents a satisfactory compromise of such claim. The operative part of the Compromise Agreement makes provision for the sale of the Rockwood Estate, and for unconditional payment of the 芒聙聹Net Liquidation Proceeds芒聙聺, as defined in the schedule, to the Government of Pakistan. The operative part further provides that the Liquidator will seek the sanction of the court to the agreement, and to the Liquidator芒聙聶s authority to enter into the same, any such sanction to be given in terms to the reasonable satisfaction of the Government of Pakistan; that the Liquidator may seek funding from the Government of Pakistan, any such funding with all amounts then advanced by, or on behalf of the Government of Pakistan to be treated in priority, and to be secured; that, subject to prior termination by effluxion of time, or as otherwise provided, the Compromise Agreement shall continue in full force until such time as all outstanding obligations have been fully discharged and the winding up of the Companies completed, but upon prior termination, the Government of Pakistan shall be entitled to proceed with its claim. The schedule makes provision for the payment to the Government of Pakistan of the 芒聙聹Net Liquidation Proceeds芒聙聺, being the proceeds of sale of the Rockwood Estate, and all other assets of the Companies, after repayment of all amounts paid by way of funding; deduction of certain fees, and expenses; the Liquidator芒聙聶s 芒聙聹reasonable remuneration in an amount not exceeding 拢250,000芒聙聺; and payment of accepted proofs of debt from creditors, but not to exceed to Mr. and Mrs. Bancroft 拢50,000, to Grantbridge Limited 拢725,000, and to all other creditors in aggregate 拢100,000 without the prior written agreement of the Government of Pakistan.




[18] The Compromise Agreement was entered into by the Liquidator and the Government of Pakistan on 4th November 2003. By letter (the Side Letter) of even date therewith, the Liquidator undertook that, save for the completion of the sale of the Rockwood Estate, he would not nor would he permit any of the Companies without the prior consent of the Government of Pakistan to pay out or otherwise dispose of any of the assets of the Companies, including the proceeds of sale of the Rockwood Estate, whether contemplated in the Compromise Agreement or otherwise until the date of the grant of sanctioning of the court of the compromise or the fourteenth day following termination of the agreement, whichever is the earlier; that, subject to funding by the Government of Pakistan, if this Court refuses to sanction the Compromise Agreement, the Liquidator will vigorously appeal against such decision, if required to do so by the Government of Pakistan, or, if any person appealed against any sanction granted, he will vigorously oppose any such appeal or application. The Liquidator also undertook that neither he nor the Companies would have any direct or indirect dealings with Broadsheet LLC, International Asset Recovery Limited, or representatives of either such companies. Such undertakings were given by the Liquidator as liquidator on behalf of each of the Companies.




[19] Although under clause 2.4, the Compromise Agreement was due to expire by effluxion of time on 30th January 2004, by agreement the same has been presently extended so that it will expire on 28th February 2006 unless this Court sanctions the Compromise Agreement, or the Liquidator芒聙聶s authority to enter into the same or such sanctioning is waived by the Government of Pakistan, and the Companies.




Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s Draft Particulars of Claim




[20] In the draft Particulars of Claim referred to in the Compromise Agreement, the Government of Pakistan refers, inter alia, to Mr. Zardari being the husband of Mrs. Bhutto, and having at various times served as a member of the National Assembly, and the Senate, and held federal ministerial positions. It alleges that Mr. Zardari was the trustee of assets and property of Pakistan as were in his possession, or control by virtue of his various offices, that he owed fiduciary duties to Pakistan, and in breach of his duties, he misappropriated and/or diverted monies from Pakistan and/or received secret commissions/bribes. 




[21] Dr. J. Schlegelmilch, a Swiss lawyer, is referred to in the draft Particulars as the 芒聙聹Bhutto family芒聙聶s attorney in Europe芒聙聺, and is alleged to have facilitated a number of banking relationships for Mrs. Bhutto and/or Mr. Zardari with certain Swiss private banks, formed offshore companies, to have facilitated the purchase and subsequent refurbishment of the Rockwood Estate, and at all material times to have been the sole director of Bomer Finance Inc.




[22] The Government of Pakistan pleads that it relies on payments that were made into bank accounts, referred to therein, and for ease herein, as the 芒聙聹Zardari Swiss bank accounts芒聙聺 in the name of Bomer Finance Inc., Nassam Overseas Inc., and Benington Management Inc., each account being allegedly controlled by Mr. Zardari and/or Mrs. Bhutto. It further relies upon certain bank documentation to establish Mr. Zardari芒聙聶s beneficial ownership of Bomer Finance Inc.芒聙聶s Citibank account, and certain other accounts within the Zardari Swiss bank accounts. It alleges that such companies were incorporated, or acquired by Mr. Zardari, and/or Mrs. Bhutto 芒聙聹simply for the purpose of receiving monies misappropriated and/or diverted from Pakistan芒聙聺.




[23] In support of its draft claim, the Government of Pakistan relies upon, and refers to alleged payments of secret commissions by Cotecna a Swiss inspection firm, which at certain times provided custom surveillance and inspection services in Pakistan, and by Soci脙漏t脙漏 G脙漏n脙漏rale de Surveillance SA (SGS), another Swiss inspection firm, and 芒聙聹parent of Cotecna芒聙聺, refers to criminal investigative proceedings in Switzerland, and to findings, sentencing, confiscation, and other orders made on 30th July 2003 (芒聙聹Swiss July Order芒聙聺) by an investigating magistrate, Judge Devaud. 




[24] At paragraphs 35 to 90, the Government of Pakistan refers in some detail to 芒聙聹relevant payments received into the Zardari Swiss bank accounts芒聙聺, to the background to the purchase of the Rockwood Estate, the promotion of the Companies, the completion of the purchase, the use of alleged secret commissions, and/or bribes for such purchase, and subsequent refurbishment of the Rockwood Estate, and to Mr. and Mrs. Bancroft芒聙聶s claim to an interest therein.




[25] In the conclusion part of the draft Particulars, the Government of Pakistan pleads that the receipt of secret commissions and/or bribes by Mrs. Bhutto and/or Mr. Zardari were in breach of their respective duties to Pakistan and/or in breach of trust; further, in the alternative, that Mrs. Bhutto and Mr. Zardari were engaged in corrupt joint enterprises involving the receipt of secret commissions and/or bribes, and that in the premises both are equally and jointly liable. Further, that the secret commissions, or bribes received into the Zardari芒聙聶s Swiss bank accounts for the benefit of Mrs. Bhutto and/or Mr. Zardari were at all material times held on constructive trust for Pakistan, that the monies and/or property received by the Companies were at all material times held on constructive trust for Pakistan. The Government of Pakistan claims a declaration that it is beneficially entitled to the Rockwood Estate, and to such other assets of the Companies as are found to have derived directly or indirectly from the alleged secret commissions and/ or bribes. 




[26] In the schedules to its draft Particulars, the Government of Pakistan sets out in some detail payments allegedly made in respect of the purchase, and refurbishment of the Rockwood Estate.




Hearing




[27] At the commencement of the hearing, leave was granted for Mr. Zardari to amend paragraph 5 of his Answer, by which amendment he acknowledged that he was the beneficial owner of the Rockwood Estate. To avoid misunderstanding, Mr. White subsequently confirmed that Mr. Zardari admitted that he was the beneficial owner of the Companies. Also, at the commencement of the hearing, leave was given to the Government of Pakistan to amend its Answer so that the same simply read that the Islamic Republic of Pakistan supports the Petition and the relief sought, and the references in the original pleading to the affidavit sworn on 23rd November 2004 (Mrs. Clough芒聙聶s affidavit) of Kathryn Louise Clough and to that sworn on 23rd November 2004 of Irfan Qadir were struck from the original Answer. I pause to record that whilst the Government of Pakistan continued to rely for support upon Mrs. Clough芒聙聶s affidavit, it had previously indicated that it did not rely upon Mr. Qadir芒聙聶s affidavit. Following such amendments the Liquidator made consequential amendments to his Reply.








MrClucas芒聙聶 opening address




[28] In his opening address, Mr. Clucas referred to the Liquidator芒聙聶s first affirmation, in which the Liquidator recorded that in, or about March, or April 2001, Mr. Paul Keating in his capacity as a director of Grantbridge Limited, a construction company, approached Maurice Raymond Dorrington, a partner in the Liquidator芒聙聶s firm, SPW Poppleton & Appleby, Insolvency Practitioners, of Finchley, London. Mr. Keating informed Mr. Dorrington that Grantbridge Limited had undertaken extensive refurbishment work at the Rockwood Estate during 1996 but had not been paid in full. He further informed him that he had met Dr. William Pepper of International Asset Recovery Limited who then represented the Government of Pakistan. The latter alleged that the Rockwood Estate had been acquired by Mr. Zardari using monies obtained from alleged bribes and secret commissions. Subsequently, Dr. Pepper informed Mr. Dorrington that International Asset Recovery Limited had been appointed by the Government of Pakistan to trace and recover assets which had been obtained by alleged corrupt activity on the part of the previous government of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, of which Mrs. Bhutto had been Prime Minister and in which Mr. Zardari had held ministerial office. It was further alleged that the Rockwood Estate formed part of the assets, which the Government of Pakistan sought to recover. As a result of such discussions, Dr. Pepper, Mr. Keating, and Mr. Dorrington met with Grantbridge Limited芒聙聶s English solicitors, Ingram Winter Green. It was agreed that the best way to progress matters was firstly for Grantbridge Limited, as a creditor of the Companies, to apply to restore Romina and Winkford to the Companies Register, such companies having been struck off for failure to comply with statutory filings; secondly, after such restoration, the contributories of each of the Companies would be invited to voluntarily wind up their affairs and appoint the Liquidator as liquidator; and thirdly, the Liquidator, in such capacity, could then sell the Rockwood Estate to realise monies for the benefit of creditors and 芒聙聹appropriate interested parties芒聙聺. I shall refer to such strategy as the 芒聙聹Strategy芒聙聺. 




[29] Subsequently, Romina, and Winkford were duly restored to the Companies Register; the Companies resolved to be each wound up voluntarily; and the Liquidator was appointed liquidator of each company.




[30] Mr. Clucas confirmed that the Government of Pakistan had ceased to use International Asset Recovery Limited relevant to its pursuit of the Rockwood Estate and any other assets owned by the Companies. On 29th October 2003, the Liquidator received a letter from Kendall Freeman, solicitors of London, to which there was attached the draft Particulars of Claim, and in which they recorded that they acted for the Government of Pakistan and that from the outset of the liquidations, the Liquidator had been aware of the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s proprietary claim to the assets of the Companies. Kendall Freeman placed the Liquidator on formal notice of the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s case, and sought that the Liquidator confirm within 芒聙聹the next three days芒聙聺 that the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s proprietary claim set out in the draft Particulars of Claim was finally accepted, failing which proceedings would issue without further notice. In a 芒聙聹without prejudice芒聙聺 letter of even date, Kendall Freeman referred to a discussion the previous day with Mr. Dorrington, and stated that they were preparing a conditional agreement between the Government of Pakistan and the Liquidator 芒聙聹along the lines discussed with Mr. Dorrington芒聙聺 to compromise the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s claim, which draft they intended to submit to the Liquidator very quickly with a view to agreeing it in the course of the then next few days. On 4th November 2003, the Liquidator executed the Compromise Agreement. 




[31] I pause to refer to part of paragraph 35, and paragraphs 36, and 37 of the Liquidator芒聙聶s first affirmation, which part of paragraph 35 commences after the Liquidator records receipt of the two letters dated 29th October 2003, and which part, and paragraphs 36 and 37 read:-




芒聙聹35. 芒聙娄 I accordingly arranged for Mr Dorrington to meet with the representatives of the ROP and the ROP芒聙聶s said English solicitors at their offices in London on 31st October 2003 to discuss their claim. The compromise under discussion appeared to Mr Dorrington, and subsequently to me, to be in the best interest of the creditors as well as allowing the liquidations to be funded to conclusion but the ROP representatives were told that I would obviously first need to seek the advice of my English solicitors (IWG) to whom a copy of the draft Particulars of Claim and a draft Compromise Agreement was sent on 31st October 2003. Documents were also shown to my solicitors and Mr Dorrington at a meeting on 3rd November 2003 which appeared to verify the movement of monies as claimed in the draft Particulars of Claim. These documents are part of the documents exhibited as bundle 芒聙聹SGT2芒聙聺. I am informed by Kendall Freeman that Bundle 芒聙聹SGT2芒聙聺 is a complete copy of the documents provided to me and/or my advisers by the ROP芒聙聶s solicitors in support of the ROP芒聙聶s draft Particulars of Claim. In the limited time allowed by the ROP to consider their claim, my solicitors and Mr Dorrington were only able to briefly review the documents made available in support of the draft Particulars of Claim. Notwithstanding this my solicitors agreed with the view reached by Mr Dorrington and on his advice by me that there was a significant risk that if ROP issued the intended action it may result in all the creditors and any other interested parties recovering nothing. It is our view that the documents made available by the ROP in support of the draft Particulars of Claim strongly suggested that Mr Zardari is the beneficial owner of the Companies and at all material times up to the liquidation of the Companies Mr Zardari and/or his agents controlled the affairs of the Estate. However the claim identified by the draft Particulars of Claim is a tracing claim and, if pursued by the ROP, either further evidence that fully traces the source of all the monies utilised to purchase the Estate may be required to be obtained by the ROP or the ROP will have to satisfy the Court that this can be inferred from the existing evidence. A key element of the claim that the ROP is beneficially entitled to the Estate is that the monies used to purchase the Estate are ultimately traceable or otherwise derived from secret commissions and/or bribes received by Mr Zardari and/or his wife Mrs. Bhutto when they held ministerial office in a former administration of the ROP. The documents reviewed by my agent and solicitors show that the monies used to purchase the Estate were received from two bank accounts in the name of a company called Bomer Finance Inc (芒聙聹Bomer芒聙聺) held at Citibank (Switzerland) SA (芒聙聹Citibank芒聙聺) in Geneva, Switzerland (see SGT2/688-702). Ms Bhutto, Mr. Zardari and Dr Schlegelmilch have been convicted in July 2003 in a Swiss Court for money laundering offences involving the companies referred to in the draft Particulars of Claim, in particular Bomer. At the time that I was presented with the draft Particulars of Claim I was shown a copy of the translation into English of the Sentencing Order of the Swiss Court issued by Investigating Magistrate Devaud on the 30th July 2003 a copy of which is produced at SGT 2/1069-1101. I draw to the attention of the Court the findings of fact made by the Swiss Magistrate that including findings that:-




(i) Bomer is an offshore company having its seat in the British Virgin Islands;


(ii) Bomer芒聙聶s representative is Dr Schlegelmilch 


(iii) Dr Schlegelmilch was introduced to the Bhutto family in the early 1980s and has been advising the Bhutto family for many years;


(iv) the beneficial owner of Bomer is Mr Zardari, husband of Mrs. Bhutto;


(v) in reality Mrs Bhutto shares with her husband the assets of Bomer over which she has power of disposition;


(vi) USD$8,190,085.00 in commissions ultimately paid by the ROP in relation to contracts granted by the ROP at the behest of Mrs Bhutto and Mr Zardari (but in the face of opposition from the Customs Service of Pakistan) to two Swiss companies were in part laundered to Bomer during the period from May 1995 to September 1997. Whilst I have not seen conclusive evidence that traces the money that was held in Bomer芒聙聶s Citibank accounts and applied to the purchase or refurbishment of the Estate to illegal commissions or bribes, the findings of the Swiss Magistrate support the view that Bomer is beneficially owned by Mr Zardari and the Bhutto family and has been used by them to launder money at or about the time that monies were transferred from the Bomer Citibank accounts to fund the purchase of the Estate.




I have recently been informed by Kendall Freeman that Mr Zardari, Mrs Bhutto and Dr. Schlegelmilch have lodged an appeal against the Swiss Magistrates decision.




36. In all the circumstances I decided to enter into the terms of the Compromise Agreement with the ROP so as to allow the administration of the affairs of the Companies, and realisation of their assets and protection of the interests of creditors and any interested parties. However this action was expressly subject to the sanction of this Honourable Court.




37. Accordingly, the parties negotiated the final terms of the Compromise Agreement (see SGT1/282-287) which is conditional upon the sanction of this Honourable Court being obtained on the 4th November 2003. I was also asked to provide a side letter agreeing to preserve the assets of the Companies until Court sanction was obtained (page 芒聙聹SGT1/288-289) in order to give the ROP the appropriate comfort they required.芒聙聺




芒聙聹ROP芒聙聺 in such cited part of the Liquidator芒聙聶s first affirmation is a reference to the Government of Pakistan.




[32] In his opening address, Mr. Clucas confirmed that prior to the entering into of the Compromise Agreement, Kendall Freeman provided some 1400 pages of documents which the Government of Pakistan stated supported their claim against the Companies.




[33] Mr. Clucas confirmed that the Rockwood Estate had been sold and certain creditors paid. During the hearing, Mr. Clucas produced a schedule which indicates that Grantbridge Limited has received the sum of 拢475,000, Mr. Essex 拢84,000, Mr. and Mrs. Bancroft 拢50,000, Shell Gas 拢5,270.78, Fedelta Managers Limited 拢25,602.26, and Eastern Arabian Limited 拢23,500. According to such schedule, Dr. Schlegelmilch has not filed a proof of debt or claim and there are three unpaid creditors namely Waverley Borough Council in the amount of 拢18,000, Inland Revenue in the amount of 拢4,950 and Customs and Excise in the amount of 拢13,000.




[34] Mr. Clucas stated that it was important that the Court appreciated the background to the Petition and referred in particular to certain paragraphs of the Liquidator芒聙聶s first affirmation, which paragraphs include indications of the issues which were required to be addressed by the Liquidator, and to dialogue with the contributories in the Companies. Mr. Clucas also referred to copy correspondence and reminded the Court that until commencement of the hearing, Mr. Zardari had been evasive as to his beneficial ownership of the Rockwood Estate.




[35] Mr. Clucas made reference to certain proceedings and evidence given in the Bow Street Magistrates Court by Richard Vincent Howard, at one time solicitor for Mr. Zardari, including evidence relevant to his appreciation of the source of the purchase monies, the source of payment of his legal fees, references to Nassam Overseas Inc. and to Bennington Management Inc. Mr. Clucas also referred to documents recording the evidence given to Bow Street Magistrates Court by Mr. Bancroft. 




[36] Mr. Clucas referred to the lack of admission by Mr. Zardari of his beneficial ownership of Bomer Finance Inc. He referred to documentation disclosed by Citibank, Geneva, Switzerland, in particular the opening of an account in the name of Bomer Finance Inc. by Dr. Schlegelmilch, in which documentation Dr. Schlegelmilch indicates that the beneficial owner 芒聙聹of the assets deposited with the bank芒聙聺 is Mr. Zardari. Mr. Clucas confirmed that it was the Liquidator芒聙聶s view that there was conclusive documentary evidence that Mr. Zardari was the beneficial owner of Bomer Finance Inc. He then referred to various bank statements obtained from Citibank, which record credits to, and payments from Bomer Finance Inc.芒聙聶s account, which payments he maintained clearly indicate that the monies from such account had been used to purchase the Rockwood Estate. Mr. Clucas then referred to further bank statements, and correspondence, which indicate that monies from such account with other payments made by Nassam Overseas Inc. and Bennington Management Inc had been used to pay for the refurbishment of Rockwood House. Mr. Clucas also referred at some length to the Swiss July Order, which includes the learned Judge芒聙聶s findings. Mr Clucas submitted that whilst the Liquidator could not say that every payment, whether by way of purchase, or otherwise relevant to the Rockwood Estate, could be traced to SGS or Cotecna payments, it was considered that the purchase monies of Rockwood Estate came from Bomer Finance Inc.




[37] Mr. Clucas then referred to documents provided by International Asset Recovery Limited to the Liquidator, including a Statement of Claim dated 3rd December 2001 (the IARL statement) with exhibits thereto. Such statement refers, inter alia, to Dr. Schlegelmilch being empowered by mandate agreements, which were signed by him as agent for declared principals Mr. Zardari and Mr. Nusrat Bhutto, relevant to Bomer Finance Inc., Marleston Securities Inc., Nassam Overseas Inc., Marlton Business SA, Capricorn Trading SA and Dorgal Associates. It refers also to an agreement between Bomer Finance Inc. and SGS, under which the latter agreed to pay a consultancy fee of 6% of the total amounts paid to and effectively received by SGS from the Pakistan Government; to a contract 芒聙聹received芒聙聺 by SGS from such Government in September 1994; and to payments made to Bomer Finance Inc. at Union Bank of Switzerland in Geneva, account 552343 on 15th August 1994 to 9th June 1995; and to payments from such account. [94] I refer to the List of Issues. Issue 1, reads:




芒聙聹1. What is the correct legal test for the Court to apply:




(i) in relation to permitting the Liquidator to seek relief in the circumstances under section 240 Companies Act 1931;


(ii) in granting leave to the Liquidator to use the powers granted under section184(1)[e] Companies Act 1931;

[95] The Companies are each in members芒聙聶 voluntary liquidation. Under section 236(1)(a) Companies Act 1931 (the Act) in a members' voluntary winding up, a liquidator has authority with the sanction of an extraordinary resolution of the company to compromise claims by creditors, or persons claiming to be creditors, or persons having, or alleging themselves to have any claim, present, or future, certain, or contingent, ascertained, or sounding only in damages against the company, or whereby the company may be liable. None of the Companies have passed the requisite extraordinary resolution to sanction the Compromise Agreement: the Liquidator has petitioned this Court pursuant to section 240 of the Act seeking that this Court sanction the Compromise Agreement, and/or authorise the Liquidator to enter into, and implement the terms of the same.




[96] Section 240 of the Act provides:




芒聙聹Power to apply to court to have questions determined or powers exercised




(1) The liquidator or any contributory or creditor may apply to the court to determine any question arising in the winding up of a company, or to exercise, as respects the enforcing of calls, or any other matter, all or any of the powers which the court might exercise if the company were being wound up by the court.




(2) The court, if satisfied that the determination of the question or the required exercise of power will be just and beneficial, may accede wholly or partially to the application on such terms and conditions as it thinks fit, or may make such other order on the application as it thinks just芒聙聺.




[97] Upon application by the liquidator, any contributory, or creditor in a members芒聙聶 voluntary winding up, the court has power under section 240(1) of the Act to exercise any power, which the court could exercise if the company was being wound up by the court. I refer to Palmers Company Law Fifteenth Edition, and that part at page 456, which reads:




芒聙聹Powers of Liquidators.




芒聙娄If [the liquidator] requires at any time the advice or protection of the Court, he can apply for it under section 252 [Companies Act 1929 of Parliament]; and he constantly does so 芒聙娄in such matters as borrowing, bringing or defending actions, the making of calls, the taking of misfeasance proceedings, compromises,adjusting the rights of contributories, adjudicating on disputed claims, settling the list of contributories, the payment of dividends, and many other like matters. Any contributory, or creditor, or the liquidator may also apply under the section to the Court.芒聙聺




Section 252 Companies Act 1929 of Parliament had the same wording as section 240 of the Act.




[98] In this case the Liquidator is not seeking that this Court determines any question, but that it exercises the power given under section 184(1)(e) of the Act in the case of a winding up by the court, that is that this Court sanctions the Compromise Agreement, and/or authorises the Liquidator to enter into, and implement the terms of the same.




[99] What is the correct test for the Court to apply when presented with such an application?




[100] In a voluntary winding up, whether members', or creditors', upon application under section 240(1) by a liquidator, a contributory or creditor, section 240(2) determines the court芒聙聶s role. The court must first determine whether it is satisfied that it is just, and beneficial to exercise the requisite power. If it is so satisfied, then the court has a discretion to determine whether it accedes in whole, or in part to the application, and what, if any, terms, and conditions ought to be imposed. If the court is not satisfied that it is just, and beneficial to exercise the requisite power, then the court can make such other order on the application as it thinks just.




[101] In determining whether to exercise the requisite power, and if so, whether in whole, or partially, and whether subject to any terms, and/or conditions, the court may be assisted by factors recognised as relevant to the exercise of the relevant power in a winding up by the court: in this case by factors relevant to the exercise of the court芒聙聶s power under section 184(1)(e) of the Act. As to the exercise of such power, I refer to Re Greenhaven Motors Limited (in liquidation) [1999] 1 BCLC 635 (Court of Appeal) and in particular that part of the judgment of Chadwick LJ at 642a to 643h. However, although the court may be assisted by such factors, when determining an application under section 240, it cannot be constrained by the same.

[104] In this case, Mr. Zardari does not make application under section 240 of the Act. He is a party to these proceedings, and has been a party since the commencement thereof. [107] In considering the weight to give to the evidence, arguments and submissions for, and on behalf of Mr. Zardari, I refer to those parts of the judgment of Chadwick LJ in Re Greenhaven MotorsLimited at page 642 paragraphs e to g and 643 paragraphs d and f, which parts read:-




芒聙聹芒聙娄 The court may, and usually will, take into account the views of someone claiming to be a creditor or contributory, but it is not bound by those views. If the claim appears thin, or the claimant can be seen to have no real interest in the assets having regard to prior claims, his views may carry little weight. I would think it inappropriate for the court to embark, in the context of an application under s 167(1)(a) of the Act, on a detailed examination of the question whether a person wishing to be heard is indeed a creditor or a contributory. The circumstances in the present case demonstrate that such examination is likely to prove inconclusive. I think it is sufficient that the court should be satisfied that the claim is made bona fide and it is not plainly misconceived. If the claimant satisfies that test, then he should be heard. It remains a matter for the court what weight should be given to his wishes.


芒聙娄


In deciding whether or not to sanction a proposed compromise the court must consider whether the interests of those, whether creditors or contributories, who have a real interest in the assets of a company in liquidation are likely to be best served 芒聙娄




In reaching that decision, the court may have to weigh the different interests of creditors and contributories and, perhaps, the different interests of preferential and non-preferential creditors. It will not give weight to the wishes of those who will be unaffected whichever way the decision goes;芒聙聺.




[108] I also refer to that part of the judgment of Sir Andrew Morritt V-C in Re Barings Plc (in liquidation) (No. 7) (2002) 1 BCLC 401 at 412 paragraph 39, which reads:-




芒聙聹 [39] Accordingly, whichever way one approaches the problem the preference shareholders interest is theoretical only. They have no commercial or economic interest in the assets of PLC available for distribution in the liquidation.芒聙聺




[109] In this case, Mr. Zardari undoubtedly has a real interest in the Companies and their underlying assets, his interest is not merely theoretical. The Liquidator accepted that the first two stages of the Strategy had been financed by International Asset Recovery Limited. It is clear that the Petition is the culmination of the Strategy.




[123] Prior to entering into the Compromise Agreement, the Liquidator did not properly consider, if at all, the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s claim. He did not consider any documents allegedly in support thereof. He did not take any legal advice. Indeed, the first time he saw the same, was when he executed it.




[124] The Liquidator agreed with Mr White that subsequent to the execution of the Compromise Agreement, and Side Letter, he was totally reliant upon the Government of Pakistan for funding, and that unless the Government of Pakistan was satisfied with the progress of matters, funding would not be available. The Liquidator further agreed that since the sale of the Rockwood Estate, all payments to creditors, legal advisers, and others had required the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s authority under the terms of the Compromise Agreement. The evidence leads to the unavoidable conclusion that throughout the Liquidator has been beholden for funding to, and influenced by the Government of Pakistan such that steps taken in the liquidations by the Liquidator have been devoid of the necessary impartiality, and objectivity, which the court expects from a liquidator. In particular, by the Side Letter, the Liquidator unconditionally bound himself to undertake certain courses at the behest of the Government of Pakistan. 




[125] The evidence further reveals that the Liquidator芒聙聶s pleadings, and affirmations are clearly open to material challenge. I refer to the above paragraph [61], and the referral of the Liquidator to paragraphs 18, and 19 of the Petition. When Mr. White challenged the Liquidator relevant to such paragraphs, there was a long and pregnant silence with no response given by the Liquidator. The description in the Liquidator芒聙聶s second affirmation at paragraph 16(vii) of payment of a sum of 拢10,000 芒聙聹interest芒聙聺 to Mr. Essex is clearly untrue. Further, such payment was paid out of liquidation funds pursuant to an agreement made initially by International Asset Recovery Limited, then by Mr Nawaz, and confirmed by Kendall Freeman.




[126] Whilst I accept that the Liquidator cannot be expected to remember particulars of each, and every claim, I find that his responses to challenges to the increase in the amounts claimed by Grantbridge Limited in the liquidations compared to those claimed in its proceedings against Mr. Pasha, to have been limited, general, and evasive. Further, in his responses concerning the 芒聙聹problems芒聙聺, which led Mr. Dorrington not to seek renewal of his insolvency practitioner芒聙聶s licence, I found the Liquidator芒聙聶s responses again evasive. In response to other challenges by Mr White, the Liquidator maintained silence, or gave a 芒聙聹no comment芒聙聺 answer.




[127] A review of the evidence, particularly that given by the Liquidator in cross-examination, leads me to conclude that he has not discharged his duties, and obligations in an independent, objective, and impartial manner. He has placed reliance, indeed perhaps over-reliance, upon Mr. Dorrington. 




[128] I find that, in determining the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s case, and the evidence in support thereof, and in pursuing this application the Liquidator has been unduly partial to the Government of Pakistan芒聙聶s position. 




[129] I again refer to Re Greenhaven Motors Limited and that part of the judgment of Chadwick LJ in which he agreed with the approach identified by Lightman J in Re Edennote Limited (No. 2) (1997) 2 BCLC 89 at 92 in which the latter said:-




芒聙聹芒聙聶 Where a liquidator seeks the sanction of the court and takes the view that a compromise is in the best interest of the creditors, in any ordinary case, where (as in this case) there is no suggestion of lack of good faith by the liquidator or that he is partisan the court will attach considerable weight to the liquidator芒聙聶s views unless the evidence reveals substantial reasons why it should not do so, or that for some reason or other his view is flawed.芒聙聶芒聙聺




[130] Whilst the decision whether to sanction the Compromise Agreement is a decision for the Court, and not a decision for the Liquidator, the Court will usually place considerable weight upon the liquidator芒聙聶s views unless there is a suggestion that he lacks good faith, or is partisan, or his view is flawed or for some other reason the court concludes that it should not do so. In this case, I agree with Mr. White that the Liquidator芒聙聶s decision is of little, if any, value. 




[131] I refer to the provisions of section 240(2) of the Act. 




[132] I readily accept that, other than challenging the Liquidator, and the Government of Pakistan, Mr. Zardari has not sought in these proceedings to identify the original source of the monies used to purchase and/or to refurbish the Rockwood Estate. 




[133] Mr. Morris referred to Mr. Zardari芒聙聶s mere denials of corruption, and challenged Mr. Bakar芒聙聶s assertions that Mr. Zardari was precluded from dealing with the specifics of the issues due to the ongoing investigations in Switzerland, or Pakistan on the grounds of sub judice. In response, Mr. White referred to paragraph 12 of the affidavit sworn on 9th February 2005 of Syed Ali Bin Adam Jafri, advocate of Karachi, Pakistan, the relevant part of which paragraph reads:-




芒聙聹12. I say and opine that with reference to query at Sub-Para (c) of Para 2 above, in Pakistan the Government has instituted Reference No. 14/2001 wherein inter-alia allegation is that Mr. Asif Ali Zardari has acquired Rockwood Estate from money acquired through corruption and corrupt practices. The action which started in 1998, is still pending and it would be subjudice to give specific details at this stage in any proceeding outside Pakistani jurisdiction.芒聙聺




[134] Given the history of, and ongoing proceedings in one form, or another by the Government of Pakistan against Mr. Zardari, and his period of incarceration. I can appreciate the cautious approach adopted by Mr. Zardari, and his advisers in these proceedings.




[135] It was accepted by Mr. Clucas that there are extant proceedings in Pakistan involving the Government of Pakistan, and Mr. Zardari, which proceedings commenced in 1998, and the subject matter of which includes the acquisition of the Rockwood Estate. Mr. White strongly argued that the correct forum for the Government of Pakistan to seek to prove allegations of corruption against Mr. Zardari was before the courts of Pakistan. He supported such arguments by reference to the assertions that the alleged corrupt practices took place whilst Mr. Zardari held office in Pakistan; that the relevant laws are those of Pakistan; and to the availability of witnesses, and documentation in that jurisdiction. Whilst I can appreciate the force of such arguments, for my present purposes, I merely note the same.




[136] Mr. White identified certain matters, which he maintained ought to have concerned the Liquidator, and should concern this Court. He referred to the previous assertion by the Government of Pakistan that the Rockwood Estate was acquired by proceeds of drug trafficking, to the granting of a restraining order in this jurisdiction reliant upon allegations of such illicit activity, and to the discharge of such restraining order in 2002. In this case, Mr. White reminded this Court, the Government of Pakistan relies upon allegations not of drug trafficking but of corruption, he challenged what new evidence has been forthcoming since 2002. Mr. White again referred to two convictions of Mr. Zardari for corruption by courts in Pakistan, and that one such conviction had been overturned by the Supreme Court on the grounds of improper government interference, and the other overturned on the grounds of insufficient evidence.




[137] I appreciate that the claim by the Government of Pakistan is a proprietary claim, and presents a significant risk to the assets of the Companies. I further appreciate that, when presented with the draft Particulars of Claim, and threatened action, the Liquidator had no funds, with which to challenge the same. I accept that the Government of Pakistan has made a claim as a creditor in the liquidations, which claim has yet to be determined. 




[138] Whilst it may be easy to criticise the time restraints imposed upon the Liquidator by the Government of Pakistan for consideration of the draft Particulars of Claim, and the Compromise Agreement, I consider that, in circumstances such as this case, a prudent, objective, and independent liquidator would have not succumbed to such pressure, but would have insisted that he was given adequate time to properly consider the claim with the supporting documentation, to take appropriate advice, including most important, legal advice, and to fully consult with others, including the contributories.




[139] I am mindful of the interests of creditors. I am further mindful that, if I do not sanction the Compromise Agreement, and, if the Government of Pakistan is ultimately successful in the pursuit of its proprietary claim, the latter might seek to recoup monies paid to creditors by the Liquidator.




[140] I am aware that if I do not exercise the requested power, and the Government of Pakistan issue proceedings, then absent new funding, the Liquidator will have insufficient funds to oppose, or challenge such proceedings, and further that at present, whilst there may be what could be viewed as negative evidence, or challenges, there is little, if any, positive evidence to challenge such proprietary claim. I agree with Mr. Morris that in these proceedings, it is not for this Court to adjudicate upon the Government of Pakistan, and Mr. Zardari, but to confine itself to the relief sought in the Petition. 




[141] Before sanctioning a compromise, the Court must be satisfied as to the facts on which it is based 芒聙聯 see paragraph 584 Halsbury芒聙聶s Volume 7(3) Fourth Edition. 




[142] Upon application under section 240, the Court must be satisfied that the required exercise of the power will be just, and beneficial before it accedes in whole, or part to the application. I bear in mind the above paragraphs [100] et seq, particularly paragraphs [119], [128], and [130]. I am mindful of the subject allegations of the intended action by the Government of Pakistan, and, if the same is successful, the potential significant consequences, particularly for the creditors. I am mindful of the proceedings between the Government of Pakistan, and Mr. Zardari, and particularly those extant in Pakistan. I bear in mind the lack of any real consideration by the Liquidator of the draft Particulars of Claim with the supporting documents, and of the Compromise Agreement before entering into the same. I have carefully considered the evidence. I am not satisfied that, in the circumstances of this case, it would be just, and beneficial to exercise the required power, and to sanction the Compromise Agreement or to authorise the Liquidator to enter into and implement the terms of the same. I decline to exercise the required power.




[143] Under section 240(2), if the Court is not satisfied that it will be just, and beneficial to exercise the requisite power, it may make such other orders on the application, in this case the Petition, as it thinks just. I look to Counsel to assist as to what orders, if any, I should make consequential upon my refusal to sanction the terms of the Compromise Agreement or to authorise the Liquidator to enter into and implement the terms of the same.




[144] With reference to costs, including costs of the Noticed Party Tower Trustees Limited sought in that party芒聙聶s Cross Petition dated 13th February 2005, I await applications, and arguments.
